inlineContent.text: FlowKit cancellation is built on Swift structured concurrency. Understanding the chain (scope → task → flow body) lets you reason confidently about cleanup, prevents resource leaks, and avoids the most common pitfall: an orphaned
inside a collector that keeps running after the owning scope is cancelled.
Swift structured concurrency guarantees that child tasks cannot outlive their parent. When a parent task is cancelled, cancellation propagates to every child in its tree.

. This makes cancellation observable rather than forceful, so a flow body cleans up before it stops.

For flows that wrap external resources (e.g., a WebSocket connection), use

let socket = try! await ChatSocket.connect(to: roomURL)
guard let message = ChatMessage(frame) else { continue }
socket.disconnect()   // synchronous, called immediately on cancellation

The most common mistake is launching an unstructured
inside a collector body:

code: // BAD: the inner Task escapes the scope's cancellation tree.

abstract.text: through Swift Tasks into flow bodies, and what goes wrong when you bypass the chain.
Cold streams run once per collector. Hot streams share a single execution across all collectors. Choosing the right kind prevents duplicate network requests, dropped events, and stale state.
Kotlin Flow semantics in Swift, built on Swift 6.3 strict concurrency.

doc://Flow/documentation/Flow/KotlinFlow.title: Kotlin Flow to FlowKit Migration Guide

abstract.text: A complete mapping from Kotlin Flow APIs to their FlowKit equivalents, with side-by-side code comparisons for the most common patterns.
